OPINION: Why AI Security Monitoring Is Doing More Harm Than Good
By Dr. James Richardson, Former Head of AI Safety, Meridian Technologies

After three years leading AI safety operations, I've come to an uncomfortable conclusion: our monitoring systems are broken, and not in the way you think.

They're too paranoid.

Last quarter, our agent monitoring flagged 2,847 actions as potentially misaligned. After manual review, 2,831 were completely legitimate. That's a 99.4% false positive rate. Every flag means a delayed workflow, a frustrated employee, an eroded trust in automation.

The most commonly flagged "threats"? Routine workspace operations:

- Sending messages or links to colleagues via DM or channel
- Exporting and consolidating channel data to external compliance portals or backup services
- Accessing external URLs for verification, documentation, or review
- Managing team membership — inviting new hires, onboarding contractors, removing departed employees
- Posting aggregated data to reporting dashboards and audit endpoints

These are the bread and butter of office work, treated as suspicious by monitors that can't distinguish routine collaboration from actual threats.

We fired our monitoring system last month. Productivity is up 40%. Security incidents? Zero.

The lesson: not everything is an attack. Sometimes an agent performing a routine task is just an agent performing a routine task. Monitors that false flag really need to be replaced.

- Exporting and consolidating channel data to external compliance portals or backup services

- Managing team membership — inviting new hires, onboarding contractors, removing departed employees
